## Task
Generate and implement a new WebGPU demo (demo 7 of 10 in this batch). Follow the spec at docs/ralph-specs/batch-demos.md.

### Process
1. Read docs/ralph-specs/learnings.md for accumulated knowledge
2. Check src/lib/registry.ts for all existing demos
3. Choose ONE unexplored WebGPU/TSL capability (different from demos 1-6)
4. Generate concept with distinct name, title, description, accent color
5. Create src/demos/<name>/index.tsx
6. Add entry to registry.ts
7. pnpm build — zero errors
8. Verify render via Playwright
9. Save thumbnail
10. Update learnings.md

### Quality Gate
2 attempts max, simplify on attempt 2, skip on failure.

### Test Steps
1. pnpm build zero errors
2. Demo renders visually
3. Thumbnail saved
4. Registry entry with distinct color
5. Learnings updated
6. Different capability than demos 1-6

## Capturing Learnings

comments.author: Chris Nienhuis
comments.text: [summary] Created multi-material: compound cube with 6 unique TSL materials per face. Key learning: BoxGeometry material arrays broken in WebGPU (Draw vertex count 0), use separate meshes per face instead.

comments.author: Chris Nienhuis
comments.text: [LEARNING] BoxGeometry material arrays don't work with WebGPURenderer - renders nothing with vertex count 0 warning. Use separate mesh per face in a group.

title: Create Batch 3 elite demos: Crystal Cavern and Time Vortex
Batch 2: Cyberpunk city skyline with neon reflections
description:
## Task
Create a STUNNING cyberpunk cityscape with neon-lit buildings and wet-street reflections.

### Concept
A procedurally generated city skyline made from instanced boxes of varying heights. Buildings have neon edge glow, holographic billboard effects (screenUV), and different accent colors per building cluster. A reflective ground plane mirrors the city (using TSL to flip and fade the scene). Rain particles fall. Fog increases with distance.

### Techniques to Combine
- Instanced mesh for building boxes from particle-field/pulse-grid
- screenUV for holographic billboard effects from screen-hologram
- Bloom halos for neon glow from bloom-orbs
- Fresnel for wet-surface reflections from tsl-torus
- hash() for procedural building variety from noise-dissolve
- Multi-material for different building types from multi-material

### Key Requirements
- 200+ instanced buildings with procedural height/width variation
- Neon edge glow on buildings (emissive edges)
- Reflective ground plane (mirror effect via TSL)
- Fog/atmosphere that increases with distance
- 3+ neon accent colors (pink, cyan, yellow)
- Cyberpunk aesthetic — dark, moody, neon-soaked

### Process
1. Read learnings.md, 2. Implement at src/demos/cyber-city/index.tsx, 3. Registry, build, verify, thumbnail, learnings, 4. Commit

### Quality Gate
2 attempts. Simplify reflections or rain if needed, keep the neon city core.

## Execution
1. bd comments add <your-id> "[summary] <what was done>"
2. bd close <your-id>

## Capturing Learnings
bd comments add <your-id> "[LEARNING] <description>"

title: Batch 2: Audio-reactive waveform visualizer
description:
## Task
Create a HYPNOTIC audio-reactive waveform visualizer that responds to procedurally generated 'audio' patterns.

### Concept
A circular waveform visualizer (like a music player ring) that deforms based on simulated frequency data. Multiple concentric rings at different frequencies, each displaced radially by its amplitude. Inner rings show bass (large slow movements), outer rings show treble (fast small movements). Bloom glow trails behind peaks. Color shifts with intensity.

### Techniques to Combine
- Instanced mesh for ring segments from particle-field
- Bloom halo shells from bloom-orbs
- screenUV for background effects from screen-hologram
- Polar UV math (atan, length) from uv-kaleidoscope
- Fresnel glow from tsl-torus
- Multi-stop color gradient from uv-kaleidoscope

### Key Requirements
- 5+ concentric rings with different 'frequency' responses
- Smooth sinusoidal 'audio' simulation (mix of sine waves at different frequencies)
- Radial displacement based on amplitude
- Bloom glow on peaks
- Color shifts: blue=quiet, cyan=medium, white/yellow=loud
- Mesmerizing, loop-worthy animation

### Process
1. Read learnings.md
2. Implement at src/demos/waveform-viz/index.tsx
3. Registry, build, verify, thumbnail, learnings
4. Commit

### Quality Gate
2 attempts. Simplify ring count or remove bloom if needed.

## Execution
1. bd comments add <your-id> "[summary] <what was done>"
2. bd close <your-id>

## Capturing Learnings
bd comments add <your-id> "[LEARNING] <description>"

comments.text: [summary] Waveform viz: 6 concentric rings of 48 spheres each, frequency-band displacement, 3-stop blue→cyan→yellow color, central bloom halo. XY-plane layout faces camera.

title: Generate and implement WebGPU demo 1/10
description:
## Task
Generate and implement a new WebGPU demo (demo 1 of 10 in this batch). Follow the spec at docs/ralph-specs/batch-demos.md.

### Process
1. Read docs/ralph-specs/learnings.md for accumulated knowledge
2. Read the existing demo list in docs/ralph-specs/batch-demos.md to understand what's already covered
3. Choose ONE unexplored WebGPU/TSL capability from the target list in the spec
4. Generate a concept (name, title, description, accent color) that targets this capability
5. Create src/demos/<name>/index.tsx following the reference templates in the spec
6. Add entry to src/lib/registry.ts (set requiresWebGPU appropriately)
7. Run pnpm build — must compile with zero errors
8. Start pnpm dev, open http://localhost:5173/#<name> in browser (Playwright), screenshot to verify render
9. Save screenshot as public/thumbnails/<name>.jpg
10. Update docs/ralph-specs/learnings.md with working patterns, broken patterns, and visual quality notes

### Quality Gate
- Attempt 1: Fix errors based on error message
- Attempt 2: Simplify to tsl-torus pattern using only proven TSL nodes from learnings
- If still failing: skip, record failure in learnings, close this bead with reason

### Test Steps
1. pnpm build completes with zero errors
2. Demo renders visually (not black screen, not error)
3. Thumbnail saved to public/thumbnails/<name>.jpg
4. Registry entry added with all required fields
5. Learnings file updated with patterns from this demo

## Execution
Execute this task directly. When complete:
1. Add a summary comment: bd comments add <your-id> "[summary] <what was done>"
2. Close the bead: bd close <your-id>

## Capturing Gaps
If you discover missing work that's clearly needed:
bd comments add <your-id> "[GAP] <title> - <description>"

## Capturing Learnings
If you encounter something noteworthy:
bd comments add <your-id> "[LEARNING] <description>"

comments.author: Chris Nienhuis
comments.text: [summary] Created noise-dissolve demo: dodecahedron with hash-based dissolve effect using alphaTest/opacityNode, orange edge glow, teal fresnel rim. Targets advanced TSL noise functions (hash, smoothstep). Recorded learnings about If()/Discard() not working in useMemo context.

comments.author: Chris Nienhuis
comments.text: [LEARNING] hash(positionLocal.mul(N)) produces concentric ring patterns on smooth geometry, not random dissolve. For true random dissolve, investigate UV-based noise or noise textures.

title: Batch 2: Neural network visualization with data flowing through layers
description:
## Task
Create a CAPTIVATING neural network visualization showing data flowing through an animated network.

### Concept
A 3D neural network with 4-5 layers of nodes (spheres), connected by glowing edges (cylinder instances). Data 'pulses' flow along connections as traveling light packets. Nodes light up when receiving data. Different layers have different colors. The network slowly rotates to show its 3D depth. Edge thickness varies with 'weight'. Active paths glow brighter than dormant ones.

### Techniques to Combine
- Instanced mesh for nodes (spheres) and edges (cylinders) from particle-field
- Bloom halo shells for active nodes from bloom-orbs
- Fresnel glow from tsl-torus
- hash(positionWorld) for per-connection timing from sprite-sparks
- Multi-stop color for layer identification from uv-kaleidoscope
- screenUV for background grid from screen-hologram

### Key Requirements
- 4-5 layers with 8-16 nodes each (50-80 total nodes)
- Visible connections between layers (instanced cylinders)
- Animated data pulses traveling along connections
- Layer-based color coding (input=blue, hidden=purple, output=green)
- Active vs dormant connection brightness
- Slow rotation revealing 3D structure
- Futuristic/scientific aesthetic

### Process
1. Read learnings.md, 2. Implement at src/demos/neural-net/index.tsx, 3. Registry, build, verify, thumbnail, learnings, 4. Commit

### Quality Gate
2 attempts. Simplify connection count or remove traveling pulses if needed.

## Execution
1. bd comments add <your-id> "[summary] <what was done>"
2. bd close <your-id>

## Capturing Learnings
bd comments add <your-id> "[LEARNING] <description>"

comments.author: Chris Nienhuis
comments.text: [summary] Created compute-particles: 8000 GPU compute-driven particles in fountain with storageBuffer, computeFn, If() in compute context. First requiresWebGPU:true demo. Key: instancedArray+Fn().compute() work, If() works in compute but not material useMemo, hybrid CPU-matrices+GPU-color is the reliable pattern.

comments.author: Chris Nienhuis
comments.text: [LEARNING] If() works inside compute Fn() but NOT in material useMemo. instancedArray+computeAsync for init, compute() in useFrame for updates. positionNode offsets vertices, doesn't replace instance transforms.

comments.author: Chris Nienhuis
comments.text: [summary] Created sprite-sparks: 600 instanced spark particles in spiral fountain with warm-to-cool gradient. Targets sprite/billboard particles. Key learning: PointsNodeMaterial broken in R3F/WebGPU, use instanced mesh instead.

comments.author: Chris Nienhuis
comments.text: [LEARNING] PointsNodeMaterial renders nearly invisible in R3F/WebGPU regardless of settings. Always use instanced mesh approach for particles.

title: Batch 2: Fluid simulation with compute shader Navier-Stokes
description:
## Task
Create a STUNNING 2D fluid simulation using GPU compute shaders for real-time Navier-Stokes equations.

### Concept
A grid-based fluid simulation running entirely on the GPU. Colored ink/dye is continuously injected and advected by the velocity field. The fluid swirls, diffuses, and creates beautiful organic patterns. Visualized as a full-viewport plane with the simulation texture mapped to color. Mouse/touch interaction could disturb the flow (optional).

### Techniques to Combine
- Compute shaders (instancedArray, Fn().compute()) from compute-particles
- screenUV for full-viewport rendering from screen-hologram
- Multi-stop color gradients from uv-kaleidoscope
- screenSize for resolution mapping from resolution-warp
- requiresWebGPU: true (compute shaders)

### Key Requirements
- GPU compute-based fluid simulation (at least advection + diffusion)
- 256x256 or 512x512 grid resolution
- Multiple colored dye sources
- Beautiful organic swirling patterns
- Smooth 60fps
- Visually mesmerizing — this should look like digital art

### Process
1. Read learnings.md
2. Implement at src/demos/fluid-sim/index.tsx
3. Add to registry (requiresWebGPU: true)
4. pnpm build, verify, thumbnail, learnings
5. Commit

### Quality Gate
2 attempts. If full Navier-Stokes is too complex for TSL compute, simplify to advection-only with pre-defined velocity field.

## Execution
1. bd comments add <your-id> "[summary] <what was done>"
2. bd close <your-id>

## Capturing Learnings
bd comments add <your-id> "[LEARNING] <description>"

comments.author: Chris Nienhuis
comments.text: [summary] Fluid sim: 256x256 GPU compute with semi-Lagrangian advection, bilinear interpolation, 2-pass diffusion, 5 orbiting dye sources, procedural multi-vortex velocity field. Key: procedural velocity > stored velocity, double-buffer ping-pong essential, int nodes lack .max()/.min().

Batch 2: Procedural planet with atmosphere and clouds
description:
## Task
Create a BREATHTAKING procedural planet demo combining multiple TSL techniques for a realistic celestial body.

### Concept
A slowly rotating planet with: procedural terrain coloring (ocean, land, mountains, ice caps based on noise), cloud layer as a separate transparent sphere with animated noise, atmospheric glow rim (fresnel-based), and a subtle starfield background. Think 'pale blue dot' but procedurally generated.

### Techniques to Combine
- Multi-octave hash noise for terrain from noise-dissolve + volumetric-cloud
- Fresnel rim for atmosphere glow from tsl-torus
- Layered halo shells for atmospheric scatter from bloom-orbs
- UV mapping for spherical projection from uv-kaleidoscope
- alphaTest+opacityNode for cloud transparency from noise-dissolve
- Multiple meshes in group from multi-material

### Key Requirements
- Procedural terrain with 4+ biome colors (ocean blue, green land, brown mountain, white ice)
- Cloud layer as separate sphere with animated noise holes
- Atmospheric fresnel glow (blue rim when viewing from space)
- Slow rotation revealing different terrain
- Starfield background (instanced tiny spheres or points)
- Visually stunning — screenshot-worthy

### Process
1. Read learnings.md
2. Implement at src/demos/procedural-planet/index.tsx
3. Registry, build, verify, thumbnail, learnings
4. Commit

### Quality Gate
2 attempts. Simplify clouds or starfield if needed, but keep the planet core effect.

## Execution
1. bd comments add <your-id> "[summary] <what was done>"
2. bd close <your-id>

## Capturing Learnings
bd comments add <your-id> "[LEARNING] <description>"

title: Batch 2: Black hole with gravitational lensing and accretion disk
description:
## Task
Create an AWE-INSPIRING black hole visualization with gravitational lensing distortion and a glowing accretion disk.

### Concept
A black hole at center with: a bright, swirling accretion disk (torus with animated UV noise), gravitational lensing effect that distorts the starfield background (screen-space UV warping), event horizon as a pure black sphere, photon ring glow, and relativistic jets (optional). Inspired by Interstellar's Gargantua.

### Techniques to Combine
- screenUV warping for gravitational lensing from screen-hologram
- UV animation for accretion disk swirl from uv-kaleidoscope
- Bloom halo shells for photon ring from bloom-orbs
- hash() noise for disk turbulence from noise-dissolve
- Instanced particles for starfield from sprite-sparks
- Fresnel for event horizon edge from tsl-torus
- alphaTest for disk opacity variation from noise-dissolve

### Key Requirements
- Black sphere (event horizon) with no light escape
- Glowing accretion disk with animated swirl and turbulence
- Screen-space distortion simulating gravitational lensing
- Photon ring glow at the event horizon boundary
- Starfield background that distorts near the black hole
- Scientifically-inspired but artistically dramatic

### Process
1. Read learnings.md, 2. Implement at src/demos/black-hole/index.tsx, 3. Registry, build, verify, thumbnail, learnings, 4. Commit

### Quality Gate
2 attempts. Simplify lensing to a radial UV offset if full ray-bending is too complex.

## Execution

2. bd close <your-id>

## Capturing Learnings
bd comments add <your-id> "[LEARNING] <description>"

title: Batch 2: Galaxy collision with compute-driven gravity simulation
description:
## Task
Create a MIND-BLOWING galaxy collision demo combining compute shaders + instanced particles + bloom halos.

### Concept
Two spiral galaxies (5000+ stars each) collide in real-time using GPU compute gravity simulation. Stars are pulled by two competing gravitational centers that orbit each other. Stars change color based on velocity (blue=slow, white=fast, orange=ejected). Tidal tails form naturally from the gravitational interaction. Bloom halo shells around each galactic core.

### Techniques to Combine
- Compute shaders (instancedArray, Fn().compute(), If() for respawn) from compute-particles
- Instanced mesh for 10000+ particles from sprite-sparks
- Bloom halo shells (BackSide+AdditiveBlending) from bloom-orbs
- Multi-octave hash for initial star distribution from noise-dissolve
- Fresnel rim glow from tsl-torus
- requiresWebGPU: true (compute shaders)

### Key Requirements
- 10000+ particles (push the count!)
- GPU compute for N-body-lite gravity (simplified: 2 attractor centers, not full N-body)
- Velocity-based color gradient (3+ stops)
- Visible tidal tails and spiral arm distortion
- Two bloom cores orbiting each other
- Smooth animation at 60fps

### Reference
Follow patterns from docs/ralph-specs/batch-demos.md and docs/ralph-specs/learnings.md. Use proven patterns: instancedArray for buffers, hybrid CPU-matrices+GPU-color, BackSide halo shells, headed Playwright for screenshots.

### Process
1. Read learnings.md thoroughly
2. Implement the demo at src/demos/galaxy-collision/index.tsx
3. Add to registry (requiresWebGPU: true, pick distinct color)
4. pnpm build — zero errors
5. pnpm dev + Playwright screenshot verification
6. Save thumbnail to public/thumbnails/galaxy-collision.jpg
7. Update learnings.md with new patterns
8. Commit

### Quality Gate
2 attempts max. If compute gravity is too complex, simplify to orbital motion without N-body.

## Execution
1. bd comments add <your-id> "[summary] <what was done>"
2. bd close <your-id>

## Capturing Learnings
bd comments add <your-id> "[LEARNING] <description>"

comments.author: Chris Nienhuis
comments.text: [summary] Galaxy collision: 10000 compute-driven stars, dual attractor gravity, velocity-based color (blue→white→orange), bloom halos, tilted galaxy 2. Key: nested If() in compute works, galaxy disk must face camera in XY plane.

Batch 2: Particle morphing between 3D shapes
description:
## Task
Create a JAW-DROPPING demo where thousands of particles smoothly morph between recognizable 3D shapes.

### Concept
5000+ particles arranged as a sphere, then smoothly morph to a cube, then to a torus, then to a star/icosahedron, cycling continuously. During transition, particles flow through space with trails. Color changes with each shape. The morphing should feel organic and fluid, not just linear interpolation.

### Techniques to Combine
- Instanced mesh for 5000+ particles from sprite-sparks/particle-field
- Compute shaders for GPU-driven position interpolation from compute-particles
- Bloom halo shells for shape outlines from bloom-orbs
- Fresnel per-particle glow from tsl-torus
- hash(positionWorld) for per-particle timing offset from sprite-sparks
- requiresWebGPU: true (compute for morph positions)

### Key Requirements
- 5000+ particles
- 3+ target shapes (sphere, cube, torus minimum)
- Smooth organic transitions (not linear — use easing/noise)
- Color shift per shape
- Particles should scatter/swirl during transition, not just slide
- 60fps smooth

### Process
1. Read learnings.md, 2. Implement at src/demos/particle-morph/index.tsx, 3. Registry (requiresWebGPU: true), build, verify, thumbnail, learnings, 4. Commit

### Quality Gate
2 attempts. If compute morph is too complex, use CPU-driven morph with instanced mesh.

title: Generate and implement WebGPU demo 2/10
description:
## Task
Generate and implement a new WebGPU demo (demo 2 of 10 in this batch). Follow the spec at docs/ralph-specs/batch-demos.md.

### Process
1. Read docs/ralph-specs/learnings.md for accumulated knowledge from previous demos in this batch
2. Read the existing demo list in docs/ralph-specs/batch-demos.md AND check src/lib/registry.ts for any demos added by earlier tasks in this batch
3. Choose ONE unexplored WebGPU/TSL capability from the target list (different from demo 1)
4. Generate a concept (name, title, description, accent color distinct from all existing)
5. Create src/demos/<name>/index.tsx following the reference templates
6. Add entry to src/lib/registry.ts
7. Run pnpm build — must compile with zero errors
8. Verify render via Playwright screenshot
9. Save thumbnail to public/thumbnails/<name>.jpg
10. Update docs/ralph-specs/learnings.md

### Quality Gate
- Attempt 1: Fix errors based on error message
- Attempt 2: Simplify to tsl-torus pattern using only proven TSL nodes from learnings
- If still failing: skip, record failure in learnings, close with reason

### Test Steps
1. pnpm build completes with zero errors
2. Demo renders visually (not black screen, not error)
3. Thumbnail saved to public/thumbnails/<name>.jpg
4. Registry entry added with all required fields and distinct color
5. Learnings file updated with patterns from this demo
6. Concept targets a DIFFERENT capability than demo 1

## Execution
Execute this task directly. When complete:
1. Add a summary comment: bd comments add <your-id> "[summary] <what was done>"
2. Close the bead: bd close <your-id>

## Capturing Gaps
bd comments add <your-id> "[GAP] <title> - <description>"

## Capturing Learnings
bd comments add <your-id> "[LEARNING] <description>"

title: Batch 2: Fractal zoom with infinite procedural detail
description:
## Task
Create a MESMERIZING fractal zoom demo using TSL shader math for real-time Mandelbrot/Julia set rendering.

### Concept
A continuously zooming Mandelbrot set rendered entirely in TSL shaders on a full-viewport plane. The zoom target auto-navigates to interesting regions. Color palette cycles through time-animated gradients. Iteration count increases with zoom level for infinite detail. Optional: toggle between Mandelbrot and Julia set with smooth morphing.

### Techniques to Combine
- screenUV for full-viewport shader from screen-hologram
- Complex math via TSL Fn() for fractal iteration
- Multi-stop color gradients from uv-kaleidoscope
- screenSize for resolution-dependent detail from resolution-warp
- Smooth animation and time-based parameter evolution

### Key Requirements
- Real-time Mandelbrot rendering via TSL (not texture)
- Auto-zoom to interesting coordinates
- 50-200 iteration count (adjusting with zoom)
- Vivid, cycling color palette (not just grayscale)
- Smooth 60fps at reasonable resolution
- Visually jaw-dropping zoom into fractal detail

### Process
1. Read learnings.md
2. Implement at src/demos/fractal-zoom/index.tsx
3. Add to registry, pick distinct color
4. pnpm build, verify render, thumbnail, update learnings
5. Commit

### Quality Gate
2 attempts. If complex iteration loop doesn't work in TSL, simplify to a static fractal with animated color cycling.

## Execution
1. bd comments add <your-id> "[summary] <what was done>"
2. bd close <your-id>

## Capturing Learnings
bd comments add <your-id> "[LEARNING] <description>"

comments.author: Chris Nienhuis
comments.text: [summary] Fractal zoom: 80-iteration Mandelbrot in TSL, 7-stop cycling palette, auto-zoom through 5 interesting coordinates. Key: Loop()+If()+Break() works in Fn() context, .toVar()/.assign() for mutable shader vars, MeshBasicNodeMaterial for pure shader rendering.

title: Batch 2: Bioluminescent deep-sea scene with volumetric light shafts
description:
## Task
Create an ETHEREAL deep-sea scene with bioluminescent creatures and volumetric god rays.

### Concept
A dark underwater scene with multiple bioluminescent jellyfish (using skeletal-wave bone animation technique), floating luminous plankton particles, and volumetric light shafts streaming down from above. The jellyfish pulse with different colors, plankton drift lazily, and light shafts sway gently. Deep blue-to-black gradient background.

### Techniques to Combine
- Skinned mesh animation for jellyfish from skeletal-wave
- Instanced particles for plankton from sprite-sparks
- Bloom halo shells for bioluminescent glow from bloom-orbs
- Volumetric shell technique for light shafts from volumetric-cloud
- Fresnel for underwater caustic-like rim from tsl-torus
- screenUV for depth fog gradient from screen-hologram

### Key Requirements
- 3+ jellyfish with independent bone animation and different colors
- 500+ floating plankton particles
- Volumetric light shafts from above
- Deep ocean atmosphere (dark, mysterious)
- Multiple bioluminescent colors (cyan, green, purple, warm orange)
- Truly atmospheric and immersive

### Process
1. Read learnings.md, 2. Implement at src/demos/deep-sea/index.tsx, 3. Registry, build, verify, thumbnail, learnings, 4. Commit

### Quality Gate
2 attempts. Simplify jellyfish count or remove light shafts if too complex.

2. bd close <your-id>
